infobox.value: system (an acronym for **Zone Improvement Plan**[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is the system of [postal codes](/wiki/Postal_code "Postal code") used by the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") (USPS). The term *ZIP* was chosen to suggest that the [mail](/wiki/Mail "Mail") travels more efficiently and quickly[[2]](#cite_note-2) ([zipping along](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/zip#Verb "wikt:zip")) when senders include the code in the [postal address](/wiki/Address_(geography) "Address (geography)"). *ZIP+4* is a registered trademark of the United States Postal Service, which also registered *ZIP Code* as a [service mark](/wiki/Service_mark "Service mark") until 1997,[[3]](#cite_note-3) and which claims "ZIP Code" as a trademark though it is not registered.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [ZIP code](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_code&redirect=no "ZIP code"))

Numeric postal code used in the US and its territories

For other uses, see [Zip code (disambiguation)](/wiki/Zip_code_(disambiguation) "Zip code (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/USA-Stamp-1973-ZIPCode.jpg/250px-USA-Stamp-1973-ZIPCode.jpg)](/wiki/File:USA-Stamp-1973-ZIPCode.jpg)

A 1974 postage stamp encouraging people to use the ZIP Code on letters and parcels

The **ZIP Code** system (an acronym for **Zone Improvement Plan**[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is the system of [postal codes](/wiki/Postal_code "Postal code") used by the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") (USPS). The term *ZIP* was chosen to suggest that the [mail](/wiki/Mail "Mail") travels more efficiently and quickly[[2]](#cite_note-2) ([zipping along](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/zip#Verb "wikt:zip")) when senders include the code in the [postal address](/wiki/Address_(geography) "Address (geography)"). *ZIP+4* is a registered trademark of the United States Postal Service, which also registered *ZIP Code* as a [service mark](/wiki/Service_mark "Service mark") until 1997,[[3]](#cite_note-3) and which claims "ZIP Code" as a trademark though it is not registered.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Introduced on July 1, 1963, the basic format was five digits, the first designating a region of the country and subsequent digits localizing the destination further.[[5]](#cite_note-mlcdintr-5)[[6]](#cite_note-lblsccs-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 1983, an extended code was introduced named *ZIP+4*; it included the five digits of the ZIP Code, followed by a hyphen and four digits that designated a location even more specific than the original five.

Private carriers and the USPS use ZIP Codes to route deliveries. In addition, ZIP Codes have become a basis for breaking down demographic, marketing, and sales data for analytical purposes.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early postal zones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early postal zones")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/UseZipCode.JPG/250px-UseZipCode.JPG)](/wiki/File:UseZipCode.JPG)

A 1963 U.S. Post Office sign featuring [Mr. ZIP](/wiki/Mr._ZIP "Mr. ZIP")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Stamps_USA%2C_Markenheft_IMG_1699.JPG/250px-Stamps_USA%2C_Markenheft_IMG_1699.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stamps_USA,_Markenheft_IMG_1699.JPG)

A [label](/wiki/Label_(philately) "Label (philately)") inside a [stamp booklet](/wiki/Postage_stamp_booklet "Postage stamp booklet") promoting the ZIP Code



A Swingin' Six video used by the post office to promote the ZIP Code

The early history and context of postal codes began with [postal district/zone numbers](/wiki/Postal_code#History "Postal code"). The [United States Post Office Department](/wiki/United_States_Post_Office_Department "United States Post Office Department") (USPOD) implemented postal zones for 124 large cities in May 1943.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Postmaster General [Frank C. Walker](/wiki/Frank_C._Walker "Frank C. Walker") explained that many experienced postal clerks were going into the army, and the zone system would enable inexperienced clerks to sort mail without having to learn the delivery area of each city carrier.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

For example:

> Mr. John Smith  
> 3256 Epiphenomenal Avenue  
> Minneapolis *16*, Minnesota

The "16" is the number of the postal zone in a specific city.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

### Establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Establishment")]

By the early 1960s, a more organized system was needed. Non-mandatory five-digit ZIP Codes were introduced nationwide on July 1, 1963.[[5]](#cite_note-mlcdintr-5)[[6]](#cite_note-lblsccs-6) The USPOD issued its *Publication 59: Abbreviations for Use with ZIP Code* on October 1, 1963, with the list of [two-letter state abbreviations](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_abbreviations "List of U.S. state abbreviations") which are generally written with both letters capitalized.[[11]](#cite_note-USPS-11) An earlier list, publicized in June 1963, had proposed capitalized abbreviations ranging from two to five letters.[[11]](#cite_note-USPS-11) According to *Publication 59*, the two-letter standard was "based on a maximum 23-position line, because this has
been found to be the most universally acceptable line capacity basis for major addressing systems",[[11]](#cite_note-USPS-11) which would be exceeded by a long city name combined with a multi-letter state abbreviation, such as "Sacramento, Calif." along with the ZIP Code. The abbreviations have remained unchanged, except for [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), which was changed from NB to NE in 1969 at the request of the [Canadian Post Office](/wiki/Canada_Post "Canada Post"), to avoid confusion with [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick").[[11]](#cite_note-USPS-11)

[Robert Moon](/wiki/Robert_Moon_(postal_inspector) "Robert Moon (postal inspector)") is considered the father of the ZIP Code; he submitted his proposal in 1944 while working as a [postal inspector](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Inspection_Service "United States Postal Inspection Service").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The phrase "zone improvement plan" is credited to [D. Jamison Cain](/wiki/D._Jamison_Cain "D. Jamison Cain"), a Postal Service executive.[[14]](#cite_note-cain-14) The post office credits Moon with only the first three digits of the ZIP Code, which describe the [sectional center facility](/wiki/Sectional_center_facility "Sectional center facility") (SCF) or "sec center". An SCF is a central mail processing facility with those three digits. The fourth and fifth digits, which give a more precise locale within the SCF, were proposed by Henry Bentley Hahn Sr.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The SCF sorts mail to all post offices with those first three digits in their ZIP Codes. The mail is sorted according to the final two digits of the ZIP Code and sent to the corresponding post offices in the early morning. Sectional centers do not deliver mail and are not open to the public, although the building may include a post office that is open to the public, and most of their employees work the [night shift](/wiki/Shift_work "Shift work"). Items of mail picked up at post offices are sent to their SCFs in the afternoon, where the mail is sorted overnight. In the case of large cities, the last two digits as assigned generally coincided with the older postal zone number.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

For example:

> John Smith  
> 3256 Epiphenomenal Ave Apt 601  
> Minneapolis, MN 55416

In 1967, these became mandatory for second- and third-class bulk mailers, and the system was soon adopted generally. The United States Post Office used a [cartoon](/wiki/Cartoon "Cartoon") character, which it called [Mr. ZIP](/wiki/Mr._ZIP "Mr. ZIP"), to promote the use of the ZIP Code.[[16]](#cite_note-mrzip-16) The name "Mr. ZIP" was coined by D. Jamison Cain.[[14]](#cite_note-cain-14) Mr. ZIP was often depicted with a legend such as "USE ZIP CODE" in the [selvage](/wiki/Selvage "Selvage") of panes of [postage stamps](/wiki/Postage_stamp "Postage stamp") or on the covers of booklet panes of stamps.[[16]](#cite_note-mrzip-16) Mr. ZIP was featured prominently alongside musical group "The Swingin' Six" in a variety show that the post office used to explain the importance of using ZIP Codes.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### ZIP+4

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: ZIP+4")]

In 1983, the U.S. Postal Service introduced an expanded ZIP Code system that it named *ZIP+4*, often known as "plus-four codes", "add-on codes", or "add-ons". A ZIP+4 Code uses the basic five-digit code plus four additional digits to identify a geographic segment within the five-digit delivery area, such as a city block, a group of apartments, an individual high-volume receiver of mail, a post office box, or any other unit that could use an extra identifier to aid in efficient mail sorting and delivery. However, the new format was not adopted universally by the public.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

For example:

> John Smith  
> 3256 Epiphenomenal Ave Apt 601  
> Minneapolis, MN 55416-2931

Commercial customers generally apply a ZIP+4 or a delivery point code (i.e., ZIP+6) to mail as part of address normalization. They may need to do so to receive discounted postage rates.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The public does not need to write the ZIP+4 code, as mail is read by a [multiline optical character reader](/wiki/Multiline_Optical_Character_Reader "Multiline Optical Character Reader") (MLOCR) that determines the correct ZIP+4 Code from the address—along with the even more specific [delivery point](/wiki/Delivery_point "Delivery point")—and prints an [Intelligent Mail barcode](/wiki/Intelligent_Mail_barcode "Intelligent Mail barcode") (IMb) on the face of the mail piece that corresponds to 11 digits—nine for the ZIP+4 Code and two for the delivery point.

For [post office boxes](/wiki/Post_office_box "Post office box"), the general but not invariable rule is that each box has its own ZIP+4 Code. The add-on code is often one of the following: the last four digits of the box number (e.g. PO Box 107050, Albany, NY 12201-7050), zero plus the last three digits of the box number (e.g., PO Box 17727, Eagle River, AK 99577-0727), or, if the box number consists of fewer than four digits, enough zeros are attached to the front of the box number to produce a four-digit number (e.g., PO Box 77, Kivalina, AK 99750-0077). However, there is no uniform rule, so the ZIP+4 Code must be looked up individually for each box (e.g. using the USPS's official ZIP Code Lookup tool, and being sure to enter just city and state, not the 5-digit ZIP).[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Postal barcode

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Postal barcode")]

Main article: [Intelligent Mail barcode](/wiki/Intelligent_Mail_barcode "Intelligent Mail barcode")

The ZIP Code is often translated into an Intelligent Mail barcode printed on the mailpiece to make it easier for automated machines to sort. A barcode can be printed by the sender (some word-processing programs such as [WordPerfect](/wiki/WordPerfect "WordPerfect")[[21]](#cite_note-21) include the feature), but this is not recommended, as the address-to-ZIP lookup tables can be significantly out of date.

Customers who send bulk mail can get a [discount](/wiki/Discounts_and_allowances "Discounts and allowances") on postage if they have printed the barcode and have presorted the mail. This requires more than just a simple [font](/wiki/Typeface "Typeface"); [mailing lists](/wiki/Mailing_list "Mailing list") must be standardized with up-to-date [Coding Accuracy Support System](/wiki/Coding_Accuracy_Support_System "Coding Accuracy Support System") (CASS)-certified software that adds and verifies a full, correct ZIP+4 Code and an additional two [digits](/wiki/Numerical_digit "Numerical digit") representing the exact [delivery point](/wiki/Delivery_point "Delivery point").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Furthermore, mail must be sorted in a specific manner to an 11-digit code with at least 150 mailpieces for each qualifying ZIP Code. It must be accompanied by documentation confirming this. These steps are usually done with PAVE-certified software that prints the barcoded address labels and the barcoded sack or tray tags.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The assignment of delivery point digits (the 10th and 11th digits) ensures that every mailable point in the country has an 11-digit number. The delivery-point digits are calculated based on the primary or secondary number of the address. The USPS publishes the rules for calculating the delivery point in a document called the CASS Technical Guide.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

ZIP Codes designate [delivery points](/wiki/Delivery_point "Delivery point") within the United States (and its territories).

### Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Types")]

There are four types of ZIP Codes:

* Unique: assigned to a single high-volume address
* Post office box-only: used only for PO boxes at a given facility, not for any other type of delivery
* Military: used to route mail for the U.S. military
* Standard: all other ZIP Codes.

Unique ZIP Codes are used for governmental agencies, universities, businesses, prisons, or buildings receiving sufficiently high volumes of mail to justify the assignment to them of exclusive ZIP Codes. Government examples include 20505 for the [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") in Washington, D.C., and 81009 for the [Federal Citizen Information Center](/wiki/Federal_Citizen_Information_Center "Federal Citizen Information Center") of the [U.S. General Services Administration](/wiki/U.S._General_Services_Administration "U.S. General Services Administration") (GSA)[[23]](#cite_note-23) in [Pueblo, Colorado](/wiki/Pueblo,_Colorado "Pueblo, Colorado"). An example of a university-specific ZIP Code is 21252, which serves [Towson University](/wiki/Towson_University "Towson University"). An example of a unique ZIP Code assigned to a prison is 81290 for the [Federal Correctional Complex](/wiki/Federal_Correctional_Complex,_Florence "Federal Correctional Complex, Florence") near [Florence, Colorado](/wiki/Florence,_Colorado "Florence, Colorado"). An example of a private address with a unique ZIP Code is that assigned to the headquarters of [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart") (72716). They may also be assigned to a single individual, such as [Smokey Bear](/wiki/Smokey_Bear "Smokey Bear") "20252", or a program, such as the Postal Service's [Operation Santa Claus](/wiki/Operation_Santa_Claus "Operation Santa Claus") program, under which children are invited to write to Santa Claus at "North Pole 88888".[[24]](#cite_note-24)

An example of a PO box-only ZIP Code is 22313, used for boxes at the main post office in [Alexandria, Virginia](/wiki/Alexandria,_Virginia "Alexandria, Virginia"), including those used by the [United States Patent and Trademark Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_and_Trademark_Office "United States Patent and Trademark Office"). In the area surrounding that post office, home and business mail delivery addresses use ZIP Code 22314, a standard ZIP Code.

### Geographic hierarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geographic hierarchy")]

#### Primary state prefixes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Primary state prefixes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/ZIP_Code_zones.svg/250px-ZIP_Code_zones.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ZIP_Code_zones.svg)

ZIP Code zones in the United States

ZIP Codes are numbered with the first digit representing a certain group of U.S. states, the second and third digits together representing a [region](/wiki/Region "Region") in that group (or perhaps a large city), and the fourth and fifth digits representing a group of delivery addresses within that region. The main town in a region (if applicable) often gets the first ZIP Codes for that region; afterward, the numerical order often follows the alphabetical order.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Because ZIP Codes are intended for efficient postal delivery, there are unusual cases where a ZIP Code crosses state boundaries, such as a military facility spanning multiple states or remote areas of one state most easily serviced from a bordering state. For example, ZIP Code 42223 serves [Fort Campbell](/wiki/Fort_Campbell "Fort Campbell"), which spans [Christian County, Kentucky](/wiki/Christian_County,_Kentucky "Christian County, Kentucky"), and [Montgomery County, Tennessee](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Tennessee "Montgomery County, Tennessee"), and ZIP Code 97635 includes portions of [Lake County, Oregon](/wiki/Lake_County,_Oregon "Lake County, Oregon"), and [Modoc County, California](/wiki/Modoc_County,_California "Modoc County, California").

The first three digits generally designate a [sectional center facility](/wiki/Sectional_center_facility "Sectional center facility"), the area's mail sorting and distribution center. A sectional center facility may have more than one three-digit code assigned to it. For example, the Northern Virginia sectional center facility in [Merrifield](/wiki/Merrifield,_Virginia "Merrifield, Virginia") is assigned codes 220, 221, 222, and 223. In some cases, a sectional center facility may serve an area in an adjacent state, usually due to the lack of a proper location for a center in that region. For example, 739 in Oklahoma is assigned to [Amarillo, Texas](/wiki/Amarillo,_Texas "Amarillo, Texas"); 297 in [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina") is assigned to [Charlotte, North Carolina](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina"); 865 in Arizona is assigned to [Albuquerque, New Mexico](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico"); and 961 in [California](/wiki/California "California") to [Reno, Nevada](/wiki/Reno,_Nevada "Reno, Nevada").

Many of the lowest ZIP Codes, which begin with '0', are in the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") region. In the '0' region are New Jersey (non-contiguous with the remainder of the '0' area), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), the [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/U.S._Virgin_Islands "U.S. Virgin Islands"), and [APO/FPO](/wiki/APO/FPO "APO/FPO") military addresses for personnel stationed in Europe, Africa, Southwest Asia, and onboard vessels based in the waters adjoining those lands. The lowest ZIP Code is in [Holtsville, New York](/wiki/Holtsville,_New_York "Holtsville, New York") (00501, a ZIP Code exclusively for the U.S. [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service") center there).[[25]](#cite_note-25) Other low ZIP Codes are 00601 for [Adjuntas, Puerto Rico](/wiki/Adjuntas,_Puerto_Rico "Adjuntas, Puerto Rico"); 01001 for [Agawam, Massachusetts](/wiki/Agawam,_Massachusetts "Agawam, Massachusetts"), and the ZIP Codes 01002 and 01003 for [Amherst, Massachusetts](/wiki/Amherst,_Massachusetts "Amherst, Massachusetts"); 01002 is used for mail in town, while the [University of Massachusetts Amherst](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst") primarily uses 01003. Until 2001, there were six ZIP Codes lower than 00501 that were numbered from 00210 to 00215 (located in [Portsmouth, New Hampshire](/wiki/Portsmouth,_New_Hampshire "Portsmouth, New Hampshire")) and were used by the [Diversity Immigrant Visa](/wiki/Diversity_Immigrant_Visa "Diversity Immigrant Visa") program to receive applications from non-U.S. citizens.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The numbers increase southward along the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"), such as 02115 ([Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston")), 10001 ([New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")), 19103 ([Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")), 21201 ([Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore")), 20008 ([Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")), 30303 ([Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta")), and 33130 (Miami) (these are only examples, as each of these cities contains several ZIP Codes in the same range). From there, the numbers increase heading westward and northward east of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), southward west of the Mississippi River, and northward on the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States"). For example, 40202 is in [Louisville](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky"), 50309 in [Des Moines](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa"), 60601 in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), 63101 in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), 77036 in [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), 80202 in [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"), 94111 in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), 98101 in [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), and 99950 in [Ketchikan, Alaska](/wiki/Ketchikan,_Alaska "Ketchikan, Alaska") (the highest ZIP Code).

The first digit of the ZIP Code is allocated as follows:

* 0 = [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") (CT), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") (MA), [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine") (ME), [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") (NH), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") (NJ), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") (NY, [Fishers Island only](/wiki/Fishers_Island,_New_York "Fishers Island, New York")), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") (PR), [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island") (RI), [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") (VT), [Virgin Islands](/wiki/Virgin_Islands "Virgin Islands") (VI), Army Post Office Europe, Central Asia, and the Middle East (APO AE); Fleet Post Office Europe and the Middle East (FPO AE)
* 1 = [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") (DE), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") (NY), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") (PA)
* 2 = [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia") (DC), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") (MD), [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina") (NC), [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina") (SC), [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") (VA), [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia") (WV)
* 3 = [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama") (AL), [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") (FL), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)") (GA), [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi") (MS), [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee") (TN), Army Post Office Americas (APO AA), Fleet Post Office Americas (FPO AA)
* 4 = [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") (IN), [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky") (KY), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") (MI), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") (OH)
* 5 = [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") (IA), [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") (MN), [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") (MT), [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota") (ND), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") (SD), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") (WI)
* 6 = [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") (IL), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas") (KS), [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") (MO), [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") (NE)
* 7 = [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") (AR), [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana") (LA), [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") (OK), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") (TX)

* 9 = [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") (AK), [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa") (AS), [California](/wiki/California "California") (CA), [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam") (GU), [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") (HI), [Marshall Islands](/wiki/Marshall_Islands "Marshall Islands") (MH), [Federated States of Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia") (FM), [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Northern_Mariana_Islands "Northern Mariana Islands") (MP), [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon") (OR), [Palau](/wiki/Palau "Palau") (PW), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") (WA), Army Post Office Pacific (APO AP), Fleet Post Office Pacific (FPO AP)

#### Secondary regional prefixes (123xx) and local ZIP Codes (12345)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Secondary regional prefixes (123xx) and local ZIP Codes (12345)")]

See also: [Sectional center facility](/wiki/Sectional_center_facility "Sectional center facility")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/10005_zip_code.jpg/250px-10005_zip_code.jpg)](/wiki/File:10005_zip_code.jpg)

Early advertisement for ZIP Code 10005

The second and third digits represent the [sectional center facility](/wiki/Sectional_center_facility "Sectional center facility") (SCF) (e.g., 477xx = [Vanderburgh County, Indiana](/wiki/Vanderburgh_County,_Indiana "Vanderburgh County, Indiana")). The fourth and fifth digits represent the area of the city (if in a [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area")), or a village/town (outside metro areas), e.g., 47722 (4=Indiana, 77=Vanderburgh County, 22=[University of Evansville](/wiki/University_of_Evansville "University of Evansville") area). When a sectional center facility's area crosses state lines, it is assigned separate three-digit prefixes for the states it serves.

In some urban areas, like 462 for [Marion County, Indiana](/wiki/Marion_County,_Indiana "Marion County, Indiana"), the three-digit prefix will often exist in one county, while, in rural and most suburban areas, the prefix will exist in multiple counties; for example, the neighboring 476 prefix is found in part or entirely in six counties: Gibson, Pike, Posey, Spencer, Vanderburgh, and Warrick. In some cases, an urban county may have more than one prefix. This is the case with Allen (467, 468), Lake (464, 463), St. Joseph (465, 466), and Vanderburgh (476, 477) counties. Cities like [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") have multiple prefixes within their city limits. In some cases, these may be served from the same SCF, such as in [San Diego County, California](/wiki/San_Diego_County,_California "San Diego County, California"), where the three-digit prefixes 919 and 920 are used for suburban and rural communities, and 921 for the city of San Diego itself, although all three are processed through the same SCF.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Despite the geographic derivation of most ZIP Codes, the codes do not represent geographic regions; generally, they correspond to address groups or delivery routes.[[26]](#cite_note-:2-26) Consequently, ZIP Code "areas" can overlap, be subsets of each other, or be artificial constructs with no geographic area (such as 095 for mail to the Navy, which is not geographically fixed).[[26]](#cite_note-:2-26) Similarly, in areas without regular postal routes ([rural route](/wiki/Rural_delivery_service "Rural delivery service") areas) or no mail delivery (undeveloped areas), ZIP Codes are not assigned or are based on sparse delivery routes, and hence the boundary between ZIP Code areas is undefined.[[26]](#cite_note-:2-26) For example, some residents in or near [Haubstadt, Indiana](/wiki/Haubstadt,_Indiana "Haubstadt, Indiana"), which has the ZIP Code 47639, have mailing addresses with 47648, the ZIP Code for neighboring [Fort Branch, Indiana](/wiki/Fort_Branch,_Indiana "Fort Branch, Indiana"), while others living in or near Fort Branch have addresses with 47639. Many rural counties have similar logistical inconsistencies caused by the sparse delivery routes, often called Rural Routes or other similar designations.

#### International mail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: International mail")]

There are generally no ZIP Codes for deliveries to other countries, except for the independent countries of the [Federated States of Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia"), the [Republic of the Marshall Islands](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Marshall_Islands "Republic of the Marshall Islands"), and the [Republic of Palau](/wiki/Republic_of_Palau "Republic of Palau"), each of which is integrated into the U.S. postal system under a [Compact of Free Association](/wiki/Compact_of_Free_Association "Compact of Free Association").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Another exception is ZIP Codes used for overseas stations of U.S. armed forces.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Mail to [U.S. diplomatic missions](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_the_United_States "List of diplomatic missions of the United States") overseas is addressed as if it were addressed to a street address in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") The four-digit [diplomatic pouch](/wiki/Diplomatic_pouch "Diplomatic pouch") number is used as a building number, while the city in which the embassy or consulate is located is combined with the word "Place" to form a fictional street name. Each mission uses a ZIP+4 Code consisting of **20521** and the diplomatic pouch number.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-:1-30)

For example, the [U.S. Embassy in India](/wiki/Embassy_of_the_United_States,_New_Delhi "Embassy of the United States, New Delhi") has this address in India's postal system:[[31]](#cite_note-31)

> Embassy of the United States of America  
> Shantipath, Chanakyapuri,  
> New Delhi,  
> National Capital Territory of Delhi, [110021](/wiki/Postal_Index_Number "Postal Index Number")

as well as this U.S. address for official diplomatic mail:[[30]](#cite_note-:1-30)

> Embassy of the United States of America  
> 9000 New Delhi Place  
> Washington, DC  20521-9000

Individuals posted at diplomatic missions overseas are now assigned a Diplomatic Post Office address and a unique box number. The ZIP Code identifies the diplomatic mission destination and differs from the diplomatic pouch number in the example above. While delivered through the pouch system, mail to such addresses is not considered "Diplomatic Pouch" material and must adhere to the mailing regulations of the host country. An example address is:[[32]](#cite_note-32)

> JOHN ADAMS  
> UNIT 8400 BOX 0000  
> DPO AE  09498-0048

### Division and reallocation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Division and reallocation")]

Like [area codes](/wiki/Telephone_numbering_plan "Telephone numbering plan"), ZIP Codes are sometimes divided and changed, especially when a [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural") area becomes [suburban](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb"). Typically, the new codes become effective once announced, and a grace period (e.g., six months) is provided in which the new and old codes are used concurrently so that postal patrons in the affected area can notify correspondents, order new stationery, etc.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Opening a new sectional center facility is sometimes necessary in rapidly growing communities, which must then be allocated three-digit ZIP-code prefixes. Such allocation can be done in various ways. For example, when a new sectional center facility was opened at [Dulles Airport](/wiki/Washington_Dulles_International_Airport "Washington Dulles International Airport") in Virginia, the prefix 201 was allocated to that facility; therefore, for all post offices to be served by that sectional center facility the ZIP Code changed from an old code beginning with 220 or 221 to a new code or codes starting with 201. However, no new prefix was assigned when a new sectional center facility was opened to serve [Montgomery County, Maryland](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland"). Instead, ZIP Codes in the 207 and 208 ranges, which had previously been assigned alphabetically, were reshuffled so that 207xx ZIP Codes in the county were changed to 208xx codes, while 208xx codes outside that county were changed to 207xx codes. Because [Silver Spring](/wiki/Silver_Spring,_Maryland "Silver Spring, Maryland") (whose postal area includes [Wheaton](/wiki/Wheaton,_Maryland "Wheaton, Maryland")) has its own prefix, 209, there was no need to apply the reshuffling to Silver Spring; instead, all mail going to 209xx ZIP Codes was simply rerouted to the new sectional center facility.

On the other hand, [depopulation](/wiki/Depopulation "Depopulation") may cause a post office to close and its associated ZIP Code to be deallocated. For example, [Centralia, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Centralia,_Pennsylvania "Centralia, Pennsylvania")'s ZIP Code, 17927, was retired in 2002,[[34]](#cite_note-34) and ZIP Codes for [Onoville](/wiki/Onoville,_New_York "Onoville, New York") (14764), [Quaker Bridge](/wiki/Elko,_New_York "Elko, New York") (14771) and [Red House](/wiki/Red_House,_New_York "Red House, New York") (14773) in New York were prevented from going into use in 1964 in preparation for the [Kinzua Dam](/wiki/Kinzua_Dam "Kinzua Dam")'s completion.[[35]](#cite_note-postofficesclose-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Relationship with local government boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Relationship with local government boundaries")]

Each ZIP Code has one or more "postal city" names assigned to it. Since a ZIP Code is a collection of delivery points served by a specific physical post office, ZIP Codes often do not coincide with the boundaries of local government units. For example, suburban and [unincorporated areas](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") may share a postal city name with a neighboring municipality, even if no part of its ZIP Code is actually within that city.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Other uses")]

### Delivery services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Delivery services")]

Delivery services other than the USPS, such as [FedEx](/wiki/FedEx "FedEx"), [United Parcel Service](/wiki/United_Parcel_Service "United Parcel Service"), and [DHL](/wiki/DHL "DHL"), require a ZIP Code for optimal internal routing of a package.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Statistics")]

As of October 14, 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit), there are 41,557 ZIP Codes in the United States.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Due to convenience, ZIP Codes are used not only for tracking of mail, but also commonly for gathering geographical statistics in the United States by some researchers.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Chen1-41) ZIP Codes are not created for statistical analysis, and thus their use for statistical analysis is heavily criticized for numerous reasons and advised against as a cartographic practice.[[41]](#cite_note-Chen1-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Adams1-42) As ZIP Codes are not polygons, but collections of mail routes and points, they are unsuitable for publication or distribution of most data.[[43]](#cite_note-Census_Bureau1-43) Polygons for ZIP Codes are not released by the USPS and instead interpolated by 3rd party vendors.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) These interpolations introduce topological errors and are not standard between vendors.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) The USPS often discontinues, splits, or otherwise modifies ZIP Codes, making continuous space-time analysis challenging, leading to issues with both the [modifiable areal unit problem](/wiki/Modifiable_areal_unit_problem "Modifiable areal unit problem") (MAUP) and [modifiable temporal unit problem](/wiki/Modifiable_temporal_unit_problem "Modifiable temporal unit problem") (MTUP).[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40)[[42]](#cite_note-Adams1-42) As the ZIP Codes are postal routing numbers, individuals and organizations without concrete spatial locations may be given a number, making it impossible to associate demographic data with them.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) Demographic data is inconsistent between ZIP Codes, and no effort is made to ensure they are proper enumeration units for analysis.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) As ZIP Codes are not made with the same considerations as other enumeration units, and is not possible without committing the [ecological fallacy](/wiki/Ecological_fallacy "Ecological fallacy").[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Chen1-41) This again becomes an issue with the MAUP. They have been found not to have significant correlations with health indicators, which can lead to poor conclusions.[[41]](#cite_note-Chen1-41) Despite these issues, ZIP Codes remain popular among researchers in fields such as public health due to their convenience, public familiarity with them, ability to anonymize subject addresses through aggregation, and possible ignorance of more appropriate enumeration units on the part of researchers.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40)[[42]](#cite_note-Adams1-42)

In an attempt to satisfy demand "by data users for statistical data by ZIP Code area", the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") calculates approximate boundaries of ZIP Code areas, which it calls [ZIP Code Tabulation Areas](/wiki/ZIP_Code_Tabulation_Area "ZIP Code Tabulation Area") (ZCTAs).[[43]](#cite_note-Census_Bureau1-43)[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) Statistical census data is then provided for these approximate areas. The geographic data provided for these areas includes the [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") of the center-point of the ZCTAs. ZIP Codes are inherently discrete or *point-based* data, as they are assigned only at the point of delivery, not for the spaces between the delivery points. The United States Census Bureau then interpolates this discrete data set to create polygons by attempting to match ZIP Code extents with Census blocks.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) The resulting aereal units represent the approximate extent of the ZIP Code, which are combined to use for mapping and data presentation.[[44]](#cite_note-Hogan2025-44) The process of creating ZCTAs and their use for statistical analysis is heavily criticized in the literature. First, the creation of ZCTAs from Census blocks encounters issues when a Census block straddles multiple ZIP Codes.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40)[[44]](#cite_note-Hogan2025-44) Addressing this is another instance of the MAUP, and the solution of dividing aggregate units between ZIP Codes causes some individuals to fall into ZCTAs that do not match their ZIP Code.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) The creation of these units is therefore committing the ecological fallacy by attempting to disaggregate aggregate data. As ZIP Codes are not continuous, not everyone in the United States has one; there are ZIP Codes for non-populated or geographic areas, resulting in there not being one ZCTA for every ZIP Code.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) ZCTAs are not updated as frequently as the USPS updates ZIP Codes, resulting in further temporal analysis issues when ZIP Codes change during a study period.[[40]](#cite_note-Grubesic1-40) Datasets providing a similar approximate geographic extent to ZCTA are commercially available. Despite these issues, ZCTAs are still very popular with researchers in fields like epidemiology, and among government agencies, with some states employing them to publish and distribute public health data during the COVID-19 pandemic.[[42]](#cite_note-Adams1-42)

### Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Marketing")]

The data is often used in [direct mail marketing](/wiki/Advertising_mail "Advertising mail") campaigns in ZIP-code marketing. [Point-of-sale](/wiki/Point_of_sale "Point of sale") cashiers sometimes ask consumers for their home ZIP Codes. Besides providing purchasing-pattern data useful in determining the location of new business establishments, retailers can use directories to correlate this ZIP Code with the name on a credit card to obtain a consumer's full address and [telephone number](/wiki/Telephone_number "Telephone number"). ZIP-Coded data are also used in analyzing geographic risk factors, an insurance and banking industry practice pejoratively known as [redlining](/wiki/Redlining "Redlining"). This can cause problems, *e.g.*, expensive insurance, for people living near a town with a high crime rate and sharing its ZIP Code, while they live in a relatively crime-free town.

California outlawed this practice in 2011.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Legislative districts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Legislative districts")]

ZIP Codes may not currently be used to identify existing legislative districts. Although the website of the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") has a "Find Your Representative" feature that looks up congressional districts based on ZIP Codes alone, it often returns multiple districts corresponding to a single ZIP Code.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) This is because different parts of one ZIP Code can be in different districts.[[48]](#cite_note-48) One proposal to eliminate the possibility of extreme partisan [gerrymandering](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering") calls on using ZIP Codes as the basic units for redistricting.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Internet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Internet")]

A 1978 proposal for a nationwide system of community networks suggested using ZIP Codes for [routing](/wiki/Routing "Routing").[[50]](#cite_note-wilber197802-50)

ZIP Code data is an integral part of dealer / [store locator](/wiki/Online_locator_service "Online locator service") software on many [websites](/wiki/Website "Website"), especially [brick-and-click](/wiki/Bricks_and_clicks "Bricks and clicks") websites. This software processes a user-input ZIP Code and returns a list of store or business locations, usually in the order of increasing distance from the center of the input ZIP Code. As the ZIP Code system is confined to the U.S. Postal network, websites that require ZIP Codes cannot register customers outside the United States. Many sites will purchase postal code data of other countries or make allowances in cases where the ZIP Code is not recognized.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

ZIP Codes are regularly used on the Internet to provide a location where an exact address is not necessary (or desirable) but the user's municipality or general location is needed. Examples (in addition to the store locator example listed above) include weather forecasts, television listings, local news, and [online dating](/wiki/Online_dating_service "Online dating service") (most general-purpose sites, by default, search within a specified [radius](/wiki/Radius "Radius") of a given ZIP Code, based on other users' entered ZIP Codes).

### Credit card security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ZIP_Code&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Credit card security")]

Main article: [Address Verification System](/wiki/Address_Verification_System "Address Verification System")

ZIP Codes are used in credit card authorization, specifically [Address Verification System](/wiki/Address_Verification_System "Address Verification System") (AVS). When a merchant collects the entire address, the ZIP Code is an important part of AVS. In some cases, the ZIP Code is the only thing used for AVS, specifically where collecting a signature or other information is infeasible, such as [pay at the pump](/wiki/Pay_at_the_pump "Pay at the pump") or [vending machines](/wiki/Vending_machine "Vending machine").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
